rules.description: Title body over 60 chars
rules.rationale: Median in corpus is 50 chars; 90th percentile is 68. Over 60 = below P75.

rules.description: Title body over 10 words
rules.rationale: Median is 8 words. Over 10 reads as cramming.

rules.description: Title does not start with 'Show HN:' exactly
rules.rationale: HN moderators may remove the [Show HN] tag if prefix is malformed.

rules.description: Any emoji character
rules.rationale: 0 out of 999 top Show HN posts use emoji.

rules.description: Em-dash (—, U+2014) used anywhere
rules.rationale: Em-dash is the dominant tell of AI-generated text. En-dash (–) is the HN convention for title separators.

rules.description: More than 1 exclamation mark in title, or any in body
rules.rationale: Hype signal. Top posts use 0 or 1 in title, almost never in body.

rules.description: Marketing vocabulary present
rules.rationale: HN crowd downvotes promotional language reflexively.

rules.description: Generic feedback request at end
rules.rationale: Specific asks get more responses than generic ones.
blocklist: let me know what you think

rules.description: No URL in title link or body
rules.rationale: Show HN without a live demo or repo significantly underperforms. Acceptable only for hardware demos with video.

rules.description: Mentions revenue, users, or growth numbers in title
rules.rationale: Show HN is for showing the thing, not the metrics. Save for Ask HN or later updates.

rules.description: ALL CAPS words used for emphasis (not product/acronym)
rules.rationale: 19% of corpus has all-caps tokens, but ONLY for product names or acronyms (API, CLI, HN, etc.). Emphasis caps are downvote bait.
rules.check: any token matches /^[A-Z]{3,}$/ AND token NOT in acronym_whitelist AND token != input.product_name
rules.note: Provider names (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Vertex, Bedrock) are treated as proper nouns, not acronyms — they pass /^[A-Z]{3,}$/ regex by not being all-caps. No special handling needed.

rules.description: 'we' used by stated solo founder
rules.rationale: Top performers either use 'I' (215 hits) or no pronoun. 'We' for solo work reads as posturing.

rules.description: Title ends with question mark
rules.rationale: 1.6% of corpus only. Underperforms on Show HN (better fit for Ask HN).

rules.description: Body contains no URL despite repo or demo existing
rules.rationale: Even if the submission URL exists, mentioning repo separately in body gives a second click point.
rules.check: input.repo_url provided but not present in body

rules.description: Every word in title body is Capitalized
rules.rationale: Reads like a press release. Top posts use sentence case after Show HN:.
rules.check: all(w[0].isupper() for w in title_body.split() if len(w)>2)

rules.description: Body over 280 words
rules.rationale: Median body ~180 words. Over 280 reads as wall of text on mobile.

rules.description: Body under 100 words
rules.rationale: Under 100 leaves the reader without enough context to engage in comments.

rules.description: Names a specific commercial competitor
rules.rationale: Calling out a named competitor (e.g. 'alternative to Foo', 'better than Bar') is legal but invites a flame war if competitor employees see it. Reframe around what your project does, not what theirs does not.
rules.fix: Replace 'alternative to X' with the capability ('self-hosted LLM observability'). Save the comparison for the first author comment if asked.
intentional_pass_examples: Show HN: Self-hosted observability for OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini
Show HN: Drop-in proxy for OpenAI calls
rules.intentional_pass_note: Listing supported providers ('for OpenAI', 'works with Anthropic', 'wraps Gemini') is NOT a competitor callout — it is a feature disclosure. The rule fires only on rivalry framing ('alternative to', 'better than', 'unlike', 'vs').

rules.description: Non-ASCII letters outside of code blocks, URLs, or known proper nouns
rules.rationale: HN is English-only. Korean/Japanese/Chinese/Cyrillic in the title or body gets flagged within minutes. Skill must translate to English at draft time per SKILL.md output-language rule.
rules.check: matches(/[^\x00-\x7F]/) outside of fenced code blocks and bracketed URL paths, excluding allowed Unicode (en-dash U+2013 for title separator)

rules.description: Standalone AI / LLM / GPT / ChatGPT / AGI token in title
rules.rationale: Negative-corpus analysis (failure-signals.md, 2026-06-18): titles with these tokens are 4.7x more common in failed posts (21.4% failed vs 4.5% success). HN front page is AI-saturated; novelty bar is high. Lead with what the product does, not the underlying tech.
rules.fix: Replace 'an AI X' with what the X does. Example: 'an AI that journals' becomes 'a journal that auto-summarizes'. AI mention belongs in the body's 'How it works' section, not the title.

rules.description: 2026-era trend words: agentic, RAG, LLM-native, AI-powered, AI-first, vibe-coded
rules.rationale: These specific terms appear 6-7x more in failed posts. Marker of trend-chasing without a concrete artifact.
rules.fix: Remove the trend word. Describe the actual mechanism in plain language.

rules.description: Title is concept-only — no product name, no first-person authorship, no Self-hosted/Open-source anchor
rules.rationale: Failed-corpus pattern: 'Local personal data redaction for any AI tools' style. Concept without an anchor leaves the reader nothing to remember. Successful titles either: (a) name the product (capitalized non-acronym noun), (b) claim authorship via first-person, or (c) lead with a category anchor like 'Self-hosted X' or 'Open-source X' (both top winning patterns).
rules.rule: If NONE of anti_patterns match the title, fire the warn. The anti_patterns are escape hatches: any one matching means the title has an anchor. The fourth anti_pattern matches 'ProductName – ...' or 'ProductName,' style openers (Formula 2).
rules.fix: Pick one anchor: (a) prepend 'I built ' or 'Self-hosted ' or 'Open-source ', or (b) lead with the product name in Formula 2 shape ('Spanlens – ...').

rules.description: Asks for upvotes, support, or any form of vote rallying
rules.rationale: Vote rallying is the single most reliable way to get a Show HN flagged and the account shadowbanned. HN guidelines explicitly prohibit it. Mods watch for it.
rules.fix: Remove the sentence entirely. HN's etiquette is that the work speaks for itself. Stating the value-prop concretely earns votes; asking for them costs them.

rules.description: Apologizes for self-promotion or for posting on HN
rules.rationale: Show HN exists precisely so builders can post their work. Apologizing reads as someone who knows they are pushing a marketing post past the rules. Top performers state and ship.
rules.fix: Delete the apology line entirely. If the post is genuinely on-topic for Show HN (artifact you built, demo or repo link), no apology is needed.

rules.description: First-time poster or long-time-lurker disclosure
rules.rationale: Common pattern in failed posts. Adds zero information about the project; signals nervousness; invites scrutiny of post karma instead of the work.
rules.fix: Delete the disclosure. Open with the project itself.

rules.description: Self-congratulation or hyperbolic claims about own work
rules.rationale: Phrases like 'I'm super proud', 'really excited to share', 'beautiful UI' read as marketing. HN crowd discounts the claim and the project together.
rules.fix: Show the artifact and let the reader judge. Excitement about your own work belongs in the first comment if anywhere.

rules.description: Comments on the post itself instead of the project
rules.rationale: 'This is my first Show HN', 'I rewrote this post three times', 'sorry for the long post' break the fourth wall and waste reader attention.
rules.fix: Cut the meta-commentary. If the post is too long, shorten it; do not announce the length.

rules.description: AI/LLM-domain hype vocabulary
rules.rationale: Loaded vocabulary specific to the AI/LLM space. Tracked separately from generic hype-words so the list can evolve with the field.
rules.fix: Describe the mechanism. 'AI-powered code review' becomes 'an LLM reads diffs and flags X'. The latter is concrete; the former is marketing.

rules.description: Dev-tool hype vocabulary
rules.rationale: Words that signal SaaS marketing pages, not engineering work. HN engineers downvote reflexively.
rules.fix: Quantify. 'Blazingly fast' becomes 'P95 12ms on 10k req/s'. 'Just works' becomes 'one binary, no config file'.

rules.description: Generic SaaS marketing vocabulary
rules.rationale: Common on landing pages and press releases. Reads as if a marketer wrote the post.
rules.fix: Drop the phrase. State what the user actually does with the product in one verb.

rules.description: First comment under 30 words
rules.rationale: Corpus analysis (104 success first comments, 2026-06-18): very short first comments (<20 words) appear in 17% of lower-scoring success posts and 0% of higher-scoring ones. Average word count: top posts 133 words, lower posts 100 words. A first comment shorter than a tweet signals nothing worth saying — extend the body instead or skip the comment entirely.
rules.fix: Add the technical detail, cost figure, or backstory you cut from the body. Or omit the first comment entirely — silence beats a one-liner.

rules.description: First comment is mostly links with under 15 words of prose
rules.rationale: Corpus analysis (2026-06-18): within success posts, bare GitHub/link drops appear 2.3x more in lower-scoring vs higher-scoring posts. A labeled resource list (Repo: ..., Docs: ..., Demo: ...) does better than an unlabeled URL. If all you have is a link, put it in the body.
rules.fix: Either add prose context (what each link is, why it matters) or remove the comment and add the link to the body's resource section.

rules.description: First comment is only thanks, excitement, or gratitude with no project information
rules.rationale: Patterns like 'Thanks so much for the warm reception!' or 'Wow, HN is amazing!' add nothing. The top-performing first comments deliver technical context, a backstory beat, or an explicit AMA. Pure gratitude belongs in a later reply, not the seeding comment.
rules.fix: Lead with technical substance. Move any gratitude to a sentence at the end, not the opener.
